sources: Japan Ministry of Finance: FY2024 Budget (令和6年度予算)
Cabinet Office Japan: Annual Report on the Aging Society 2024
Ministry of Finance Japan: Highlights of the FY2024 Budget
Bank of Japan: Government Debt Statistics 2024
meta.notes: UBI figures assume 50% of savings distributed equally to all 125M Japanese residents. All figures converted from JPY at ~150 JPY/USD. Social security dominates Japan's budget reflecting one of the world's most rapidly aging populations.

items.name: Social Security (社会保障費 — Shakai Hoshō)
items.agency: Ministry of Health, Labour and Welfare
items.description: Japan's largest budget item covering national pension (Kokumin Nenkin), employee pension (Kosei Nenkin), public health insurance (Kokumin Kenko Hoken), long-term care insurance (Kaigo Hoken), and welfare assistance for 36M+ elderly in the world's most aged society.
items.cut_note: Japan has the world's oldest population (29% over 65). Cutting social security would devastate tens of millions of retirees with no alternative income source.

items.name: National Debt Service (国債費 — Kokusai-hi)
items.description: Interest payments and principal redemption on Japan's national debt — the largest in the developed world at ~260% of GDP (~$10T). Japan's JGB market is the world's second-largest sovereign bond market.
items.cut_note: Defaulting on JGBs would collapse Japan's financial system and trigger a global bond market crisis given Japan's position as the world's largest creditor nation.

items.name: Local Allocation Tax Grants (地方交付税交付金 — Chiho Kofuzei)
items.agency: Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications
items.description: Equalization transfers from the national government to Japan's 47 prefectures and ~1,700 municipalities to fund local government operations, schools, public safety, and social services in lower-tax rural areas.

items.name: Defense — Japan Self-Defense Forces (防衛費 — Boei-hi)
items.description: Japan Self-Defense Forces (Ground, Maritime, Air) plus new Space and Cyber domains. Japan has dramatically increased defense spending since 2022, targeting 2% of GDP by 2027 — a historic shift from its postwar pacifist posture.

items.name: Public Works (公共事業費 — Kōkyō Jigyō-hi)
items.agency: Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism
items.description: Roads, bridges, ports, flood control, earthquake-resistant infrastructure, and rural development projects. Japan's mountainous terrain and seismic activity require constant infrastructure investment and disaster preparedness.

items.name: Education, Culture & Science (文部科学省 — MEXT)
items.agency: Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology
items.description: Compulsory education subsidies to prefectures, national university operational grants, Japan Society for the Promotion of Science (JSPS) research funding, cultural preservation, and the Japan Aerospace Exploration Agency (JAXA).

items.name: Economic Cooperation & ODA (経済協力費)
items.description: Japan's Official Development Assistance (ODA) — one of the world's largest bilateral aid programs. Funds infrastructure projects, technical cooperation, and humanitarian assistance across Asia, Africa, and Latin America via JICA.

items.name: Other Government Expenditures (その他の歳出)
items.description: Remaining budget across all ministries: trade and industry (METI) subsidies including semiconductor incentives (Rapidus, TSMC Kumamoto), agriculture (food self-sufficiency programs), justice, parliamentary operations, and emergency reserve funds.
